zones.name: Asakusa & the Ancient Heart of Edo
zones.description: Home to Sensō-ji, Tokyo's oldest and most visited temple dating back to 645 AD, this historic riverside district is the spiritual and cultural soul of old Tokyo. Rickshaws, kimono-clad visitors, and the iconic Kaminarimon gate make it one of Asia's most atmospheric neighborhoods. The area also boasts one of Japan's top-rated samurai and ninja experiences, drawing millions of visitors annually.

zones.name: Tokyo Skytree & Solamachi: Sky-High Tokyo
zones.description: Anchored by the world's tallest broadcasting tower at 634 meters, this futuristic district on the eastern bank of the Sumida River offers breathtaking panoramic views over all of Tokyo. The base of the Skytree houses Solamachi, a vast shopping and dining complex with over 300 shops. It's one of Tokyo's most visited landmarks, consistently drawing over 6 million visitors a year to its observation decks.

zones.name: Ueno & Akihabara: Culture, Chaos & Cool
zones.description: This sprawling corridor stretches from Ueno's world-class museum row — anchored by the Tokyo National Museum, Japan's oldest and largest — to the neon-lit electronics and anime mecca of Akihabara. Heaven's Gate stands out as a near-perfect rated hidden gem among the area's otaku shops and arcade halls. Whether you crave Japanese antiquities or the latest manga releases, this zone delivers an unmatched depth of experience.

zones.name: Shinjuku: Neon Nights & Garden Escapes
zones.description: Tokyo's most electrifying district packs everything into one dense, dazzling neighborhood — from the tranquil 58-hectare Shinjuku Gyoen National Garden, one of Japan's most beloved parks, to the raucous izakayas of Golden Gai and the skyscraper-lined west exit. With the world's busiest railway station at its heart, Shinjuku handles over 3.5 million passengers daily and offers an extraordinary mix of nightlife, fine dining, wellness, and green space.

zones.name: Roppongi, Ginza & Tokyo Tower: Icons of Style
zones.description: This glamorous central arc unites the steel-and-glass luxury of Ginza — Tokyo's most prestigious shopping district — with the bohemian art galleries of Roppongi and the enduring romance of Tokyo Tower, illuminated nightly against the skyline since 1958. Tsukiji Outer Market and Hamarikyu Gardens add world-class culinary and natural counterpoints to the designer boutiques. With over 250 restaurants, bars, galleries, and shops, this is the city's most cosmopolitan quarter.

zones.name: Odaiba: Futuristic Island on Tokyo Bay
zones.description: Built entirely on reclaimed land in Tokyo Bay, Odaiba is a gleaming futuristic playground home to the iconic 18-meter Unicorn Gundam statue, teamLab digital art installations, and sweeping views of Rainbow Bridge and the city skyline. DiverCity Tokyo Plaza and Venus Fort anchor the retail scene, while Odaiba Seaside Park offers rare waterfront relaxation within the metropolis. It's one of Tokyo's most photogenic districts and a top destination for families and tech enthusiasts alike.

zones.name: Toyosu & Koto: Digital Art & Working Waterfront
zones.description: This evolving eastern waterfront zone bridges the gap between Tokyo's working fishing heritage — now centered at the state-of-the-art Toyosu Market, the world's largest wholesale fish market — and cutting-edge immersive art at teamLab Planets. The Museum of Contemporary Art Tokyo and the Fukagawa Edo Museum enrich the cultural offering, while Kiyosumi Gardens provides a serene classical counterpoint. It's one of Tokyo's fastest-changing and most creatively energetic districts.

zones.name: Ikebukuro: Otaku Heaven & Sky Views
zones.description: Ikebukuro is Tokyo's underrated pop culture powerhouse, home to the towering Sunshine 60 — once Asia's tallest skyscraper — and the flagship Animate store, the world's largest anime retail chain. The area rivals Akihabara for manga, gaming, and collectible culture, attracting an especially passionate domestic fanbase. Its rooftop observation park, diverse dining scene, and proximity to Zoshigaya offer a more local, less touristy alternative to its more famous neighbors.

zones.name: Nakano: The Original Otaku Village
zones.description: Nakano Broadway is the original home of Japan's vintage and rare collectibles culture, predating Akihabara's tourist boom and beloved by hardcore collectors worldwide. The Mandarake chain — Japan's largest used manga and anime retailer — was born here and still dominates the mall's upper floors with an extraordinary inventory. The surrounding neighborhood offers an authentic, unhurried slice of suburban Tokyo that feels refreshingly off the beaten path.

zones.name: Korakuen, Bunkyō & Tokyo Dome: Sports, Gardens & Thrills
zones.description: This compact district north of central Tokyo is a delightful paradox — a 17th-century Edo-period garden sits steps from a major league baseball stadium and an amusement park. Koishikawa Korakuen, one of Tokyo's two surviving Edo-era gardens, is celebrated for its seasonal beauty, particularly cherry blossoms and autumn foliage. Tokyo Dome City Attractions adds family-friendly rides including the Thunder Dolphin roller coaster that loops around the stadium itself.

zones.name: Rikugien & Kyu-Furukawa: Northern Garden Gems
zones.description: This quiet northern pocket of Tokyo preserves two of the city's finest Meiji-era gardens side by side, offering a rare double dose of classical Japanese landscaping away from the tourist crowds. Rikugien, commissioned in 1702, is considered the most beautiful Edo-period garden in Tokyo and is especially famous for its illuminated weeping cherry tree each spring. Kyu-Furukawa Gardens pairs a Western-style rose garden above with a serene Japanese garden below, a legacy of Meiji-era cultural fusion.

zones.name: Meguro & Shinagawa: Refined Culture South of the Yamanote
zones.description: Tucked south of central Tokyo, this understated zone rewards the curious traveler with Art Deco splendor at the Tokyo Metropolitan Teien Art Museum, one of Japan's finest examples of the style, set within a gorgeous garden. The Meguro River's cherry blossom promenade is arguably Tokyo's most photographed springtime corridor, drawing huge crowds each March and April. The area blends old-money residential calm with some of Tokyo's most thoughtful museum programming.

top_places.name: Institute for Nature Study, National Museum of Nature and Science

all_places.name: Institute for Nature Study, National Museum of Nature and Science

zones.name: Mitaka & Kichijoji: Ghibli Magic & Inokashira Calm
zones.description: This beloved western neighborhood is famously home to the Ghibli Museum, a one-of-a-kind experience designed by Studio Ghibli's Hayao Miyazaki himself that requires advance lottery tickets, making a visit feel genuinely special. The adjacent Inokashira Park, with its serene boating pond and woodland paths, is consistently ranked among Tokyo's top urban parks and beloved by locals for hanami season. Together they form Tokyo's most enchanting day-trip destination for families and film lovers.

zones.name: Tokyo Disney Resort: The Magic Kingdom of the East
zones.description: Located in Urayasu just east of central Tokyo, the Tokyo Disney Resort is the most visited theme park resort in Asia and one of the top five most visited in the world, welcoming over 25 million guests annually. Tokyo Disneyland opened in 1983 as the first Disney park outside the United States, while Tokyo DisneySea — widely regarded by enthusiasts as the world's most beautiful theme park — opened in 2001 with a stunning nautical design entirely unique to Japan. The resort is a masterclass in themed entertainment and guest experience.

zones.name: Nerima & Toshima: Warner Bros. Magic & Onsen Retreats
zones.description: This northwestern residential zone has been transformed by the arrival of the Warner Bros. Studio Tour Tokyo, one of Japan's most anticipated new attractions offering an immersive behind-the-scenes journey through the world of Harry Potter. The surrounding area is also home to Toshimaen Niwa-no-yu, a relaxing onsen complex built on the grounds of a former amusement park, blending nostalgic charm with modern bathing culture. It's an ideal half-day escape combining blockbuster entertainment with authentic Japanese relaxation.
